evidence: Runtime dependency injection is implemented as very large callback bags (for example `importerRuntime` and `startupInitRuntime`/`startBackgroundServicesRuntime`) with many grouped validators and default mergers, which increases indirection cost before core logic executes.

Cross-module code repeatedly depends on broad global config/runtime snapshots (`config.*Snapshot`, `config.BasePath`, `config.RuntimeContextSnapshot`) instead of narrower domain inputs, so boundaries are hidden behind shared state access.

Broadcast lifecycle exposes multiple near-pass-through `*WithRuntime` layers that mostly add nil checks and forwarding (`StartBroadcastLoop`, `StartBroadcastLoopWithRuntime`, `runBroadcastTick`, `runBroadcastTickWithRuntime`, `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntime`, `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntimeState`).

findings.summary: Monolithic runtime callback bags create high indirection and weakly bounded dependencies.
evidence: `importerRuntime` composes multiple embedded runtime structs with a large callback surface and separate validation phases (`validateImporterConfigRuntime`, `validateImporterProvisionRuntime`, `validateImporterPublishRuntime`).

`startuporchestrator` splits startup into several embedded runtime bags with many function fields and merges defaults via seam helpers, so initialization behavior is distributed across indirection layers.
`workflowRuntime` embeds `RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, and `RuntimeSnapshotOps`, making operation functions depend on a broad capability bundle even for narrower workflows.
findings.suggestion: Replace broad runtime bags with smaller phase-specific interfaces (for example per use-case: init, publish, provisioning), pass only required capabilities into each function, and remove cross-phase embedding so dependency contracts are explicit and minimal.

findings.summary: Global config/context snapshot access acts as a wide parameter bag across subsystems.
evidence: `runtimecontext` exposes process-wide mutable snapshot getters/setters (`BasePath`, `Architecture`, `DebugMode`, `DockerDir`) backed by global atomic state.
`main` and startup code pull settings from `config.*Snapshot` and runtime context globally inside default runtime constructors instead of receiving explicit config values from callers.
`SysConfig`/`UrbitDocker` remain broad aggregate structures that are read and mutated by distant modules, encouraging broad coupling to configuration shape rather than domain-specific inputs.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit, immutable configuration structs per subsystem (startup, networking, import, workflow) and pass them through constructors; restrict global snapshot access to a thin bootstrap layer and keep downstream packages on narrow typed inputs.

findings.summary: Broadcast runtime APIs contain low-leverage forwarding layers that deepen call chains.
evidence: Several entrypoints immediately forward to `*WithRuntime` variants after only nil/default checks (`StartBroadcastLoop`, `ReloadUrbits`, `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntime`).

Tick execution similarly has wrapper layers (`runBroadcastTick` -> `runBroadcastTickWithRuntime` -> `buildBroadcastTickState`) while runtime resolution is split across helper functions (`newBroadcastLoopRuntimeWithDefault`, `resolveBroadcastLoopRuntime`).
This pattern adds abstraction depth without introducing distinct policy boundaries, making lifecycle behavior harder to follow.
findings.suggestion: Collapse wrapper pairs where behavior is identical, keep one explicit injected-path API plus a single package-level convenience entrypoint, and centralize default runtime resolution in one place to reduce forwarding layers.

findings.summary: Event broker abstraction mixes delegate and in-memory fallback semantics in one type.
evidence: `EventRuntime` can either delegate to an external `EventBroker` or publish to local channels, decided per call in generic helpers (`publishTransition`, `runtimeTransitionSource`).
Callers use `DefaultEventRuntime()` directly in workflow/importer defaults, so runtime behavior depends on hidden delegate/fallback mode rather than a single explicit broker contract.
This dual behavior increases interface ambiguity because the same runtime type can represent materially different transport guarantees.
findings.suggestion: Split broker roles into explicit implementations (for example `InMemoryEventBroker` and `DelegatingEventBroker`) and inject one concrete broker per runtime so publish/subscribe behavior is fixed and transparent.
